title: Quiz: Mutual Information
description: Test your understanding of mutual information and its properties

questions.question: If X and Y are independent random variables, what is I(X;Y)?
questions.explanation: For independent variables, H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y), so I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) = 0. Independence means knowing one variable tells you nothing about the other.

questions.question: Which expression is NOT equal to I(X;Y)?
questions.explanation: I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y), not H(X|Y) - H(X). The correct formula subtracts conditional from marginal entropy, not vice versa. H(X|Y) - H(X) would give a negative value.

questions.question: For the data processing inequality with Markov chain X → Y → Z, which is always true?
questions.explanation: The data processing inequality states I(X;Z) ≤ I(X;Y) for Markov chain X → Y → Z. Processing cannot create information about the source - information can only be lost or preserved.

questions.question: Mutual information can be negative when two variables are anti-correlated.
questions.explanation: False. Mutual information is always non-negative: I(X;Y) ≥ 0. This is because I(X;Y) = D_KL(p(x,y) || p(x)p(y)), and KL divergence is always non-negative. The sign of correlation doesn't affect MI.

questions.question: I(X;Y) = H(X) when:

options.text: Y is a deterministic function of X
X is a deterministic function of Y
X and Y have the same distribution

questions.explanation: When X is a deterministic function of Y, knowing Y tells you everything about X, so H(X|Y) = 0. Therefore I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) = H(X). This is the maximum possible mutual information from X's perspective.

questions.question: What does I(X;Y) = D_KL(p(x,y) || p(x)p(y)) measure?

options.text: The average entropy of X and Y
How much the joint distribution differs from independence
The correlation coefficient between X and Y

questions.explanation: I(X;Y) as KL divergence measures how much the joint distribution p(x,y) differs from what it would be under independence p(x)p(y). Zero divergence means exact independence.

questions.question: I(X;Y) ≤ min{H(X), H(Y)} always holds.
questions.explanation: True. Since I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) ≤ H(X) and I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X) ≤ H(Y), we have I(X;Y) ≤ min{H(X), H(Y)}. You can't learn more about X from Y than X contains.

questions.question: Which statements about mutual information are correct? (Select all)

options.text: I(X;Y|Z) is always less than I(X;Y)

questions.explanation: a) Correct: MI is symmetric. b) Correct: I(X;X) = H(X) - H(X|X) = H(X) - 0 = H(X). c) Incorrect: I(X;Y|Z) can be larger than I(X;Y) (e.g., when Z explains away correlation). d) Correct: This is the chain rule for MI.
